en.extract:
D'Arnaud's barbet (Trachyphonus darnaudii) is a species of bird in the family Lybiidae that is found in East Africa. Barbets and toucans are a group of birds with a worldwide tropical distribution. The barbets get their name from the bristles which fringe their heavy bills. Three geographical races (darnaudii, böhmi, emini) have been recorded. The usambiro barbet was previously regarded as a subspecies, but the 2021 taxonomic update to the IOC World Bird List upgraded this to full species status with the taxonomic name Trachyphonus usambiro.


Taxonomy
D'Arnaud's barbet was formally described in 1847 as Micropogon darnaudii by the French naturalists Florent Prévost and Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs. The species was named in honour of the French explorer and engineer Joseph Pons d'Arnaud. The type locality is the Kordofan region of central Sudan. D'Arnaud's barbet is now one of four species placed in the genus Trachyphonus that was introduced 1821 by the Italian naturalist Camillo Ranzani.
Four subspecies are recognised:

T. d. darnaudii (Prévost, F & des Murs, MAPO, 1847) – southeastern South Sudan, southwestern Ethiopia, northeastern Uganda, and west-central Kenya
T. d. boehmi Fischer, GA & Reichenow, A, 1884 – southern and eastern Ethiopia to southern Somalia, eastern Kenya, and northeastern Tanzania
T. d. emini Reichenow, A, 1891 – north-central Tanzania (east to Dar es Salaam suburbs)
The Usambiro barbet, T. usambiro, found in southwestern Kenya to north-central Tanzania, has sometimes been considered as a subspecies, T. d. usambiro (Neumann, OR, 1908). Since 2021 it has been regarded as a separate species, the Usambiro barbet.


Behaviour
D'Arnaud's barbet is a small East African bird that feeds on insects, fruits, and seeds. It grows to about eight inches, and is equally at home in trees or on the ground.  A vertical tunnel two to three feet into the ground with a sideways and upward turn leads to the nest chamber. In a striking dance the male and female face each on nearby twigs and twitch, bob and sing like mechanical toys.
They vocalize in groups, starting with a specific vocalization described as a chewp noise, often simultaneously raising and fanning the tail.

'''D'Arnaud's barbet''' ('''''Trachyphonus darnaudii''''') is a [[species]] of bird in the family [[Lybiidae]] that is found in East Africa. Barbets and [[toucans]] are a group of birds with a worldwide tropical distribution. The barbets get their name from the bristles which fringe their heavy bills. Three geographical races (''darnaudii'', ''böhmi'', ''emini'') have been recorded. The [[usambiro barbet]] was previously regarded as a subspecies, but the 2021 taxonomic update to the IOC World Bird List upgraded this to full species status with the taxonomic name ''Trachyphonus usambiro''.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gill |first1=F. |last2=Donsker |first2=D. |last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. (Eds.) |title=Master Lists – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/ioc-lists/master-list-2/ |website=IOC World Bird List |access-date=5 August 2021 |archive-date=1 May 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210501160036/https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/ioc-lists/master-list-2/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
D'Arnaud's barbet was [[species description|formally described]] in 1847 as ''Micropogon darnaudii'' by the French naturalists [[Florent Prévost]] and [[Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs]].<ref>{{ cite book | last1=Prévost | first1=Florent | author1-link=Florent Prévost | last2=Des Murs  | first2=Marc Athanase Parfait Oeillet  | author2-link=Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs | year=1847 | title=Voyage en Abyssinie exécuté pendant les années 1839, 1840, 1841, 1842, 1843 | volume=6 | language=French | location=Paris | publisher=Arthus Bertrand | pages=133–135 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/62875484 }}</ref> The species was named in honour of the French explorer and engineer [[Joseph Pons d'Arnaud]].<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=darnaudii | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=darnaudii | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=22 January 2026 }}</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is the [[Kordofan]] region of central [[Sudan]].<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1948 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=6 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=62 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14477495 }}</ref> D'Arnaud's barbet is now one of four species placed in the genus ''[[Trachyphonus]]'' that was introduced 1821 by the Italian naturalist [[Camillo Ranzani]].<ref name=avilist>{{ cite web | author=AviList Core Team | date=2025 | title=AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025 | doi=10.2173/avilist.v2025 | doi-access=free | url=http://www.avilist.org/checklist/v2025/ | access-date=22 January 2026 }}</ref>

Four [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=avilist/>
* ''T. d. darnaudii'' ([[Florent Prévost|Prévost, F]] & [[Marc Athanase Parfait Œillet des Murs|des Murs, MAPO]], 1847) – southeastern South Sudan, southwestern Ethiopia, northeastern Uganda, and west-central Kenya
* ''T. d. boehmi'' [[Gustav Fischer (explorer)|Fischer, GA]] & [[Anton Reichenow|Reichenow, A]], 1884 – southern and eastern Ethiopia to southern Somalia, eastern Kenya, and northeastern Tanzania
* ''T. d. emini'' Reichenow, A, 1891 – north-central Tanzania (east to Dar es Salaam suburbs)

The [[Usambiro barbet]], ''T. usambiro'', found in southwestern Kenya to north-central Tanzania, has sometimes been considered as a subspecies, ''T. d. usambiro'' ([[Oscar Neumann|Neumann, OR]], 1908). Since 2021 it has been regarded as a separate species, the Usambiro barbet.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=Jacamars, puffbirds, toucans, barbets, honeyguides | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/jacamars/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=22 January 2026 | archive-date=27 April 2020 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200427191436/https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/jacamars/ | url-status=live }}</ref>

==Behaviour==
D'Arnaud's barbet is a small East African bird that feeds on insects, fruits, and seeds. It grows to about eight inches, and is equally at home in trees or on the ground.  A vertical tunnel two to three feet into the ground with a sideways and upward turn leads to the nest chamber. In a striking dance the male and female face each on nearby twigs and twitch, bob and sing like mechanical toys.

They vocalize in groups, starting with a specific vocalization described as a ''chewp'' noise, often simultaneously raising and fanning the tail.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mahamoud Issa |first1=Mathieu |last2=Sikora |first2=Bożena |last3=Rusiecki |first3=Stanisław |last4=Osiejuk |first4=Tomasz S. |date=2022-09-09 |title=The Yellow-breasted Barbet (Trachyphonus margaritatus) introduces vocal duets and choruses with a specific multimodal signal, during territorial advertisement |journal=Journal of Ornithology |volume=164 |issue=1 |pages=183–192 |doi=10.1007/s10336-022-02016-w |issn=2193-7192|doi-access=free }}</ref>
